---
title: "Cost of Living"
description: "Inflation — overall, core, and food — against the Bank of Canada target"
---

This page tracks consumer-price inflation: the headline **all-items** rate, the
Bank of Canada's preferred **core** measure (CPI-trim), and **food** — the
component households feel most.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import math
from datetime import timedelta
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import CANADA_COLOR, CATEGORICAL_COLORS, get_data_date, get_next_release
from pipeline.charts import category_bar, category_bar_views, lines_over_time, province_colors
```

## Inflation (CPI, year-over-year)

Canada's inflation rate, measured as the year-over-year change in the Consumer Price Index (all items). The grey band shows the Bank of Canada's 1–3% target range, with the 2% midpoint as a dashed line.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    cpi = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    if "product_group" in cpi.columns:
        cpi = cpi[cpi["product_group"] == "All-items"]
    cpi = cpi.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
    cpi["cpi_value"] = pd.to_numeric(cpi["cpi_value"], errors="coerce")
    cpi["inflation_yoy"] = cpi["cpi_value"].pct_change(periods=12) * 100
    cpi = cpi.dropna(subset=["inflation_yoy"])

    colors = []
    for v in cpi["inflation_yoy"]:
        if v > 3:
            colors.append("#C0392B")   # above target: alert red (a valence, NOT the Canada-entity maroon)
        elif v < 1:
            colors.append("#1f77b4")
        else:
            colors.append("#555555")

    fig = go.Figure()
    fig.add_hrect(y0=1, y1=3, fillcolor="#e0e0e0", opacity=0.4, line_width=0, layer="below")
    fig.add_trace(go.Bar(x=cpi["date"], y=cpi["inflation_yoy"], marker_color=colors,
        hovertemplate="<b>%{x|%b %Y}</b><br>Inflation: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    fig.add_hline(y=2, line_dash="dash", line_color="#888", opacity=0.7)

    latest = cpi["date"].max()
    one_year_ago = latest - timedelta(days=365)
    x_start = one_year_ago - timedelta(days=15)
    x_end = latest + timedelta(days=31)
    visible = cpi[cpi["date"] >= one_year_ago]["inflation_yoy"]
    y_min_val, y_max_val = visible.min(), visible.max()
    y_min = 0 if y_min_val >= 0 else math.floor(y_min_val * 2) / 2 - 0.5
    y_max = math.ceil(max(y_max_val, 3.5) * 2) / 2 + 1.0

    _src = (f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0004-01 | "
            f"Data as of: {get_data_date('data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv')}")
    _next = get_next_release('data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv')
    if _next:
        _src += f" | Next update: {_next}"

    fig.update_layout(
        xaxis_title="", yaxis_title="Inflation (%)", plot_bgcolor="white",
        meta=dict(autoscaleY=dict(include=[0, 3], padFrac=0.08)),
        xaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0", range=[x_start, x_end], tickformat="%b\n%Y",
            rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                dict(count=1, label="1Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=2, label="2Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.06, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        yaxis=dict(gridcolor="#e0e0e0", range=[y_min, y_max]),
        showlegend=False, margin=dict(b=150, t=40, r=80),
        annotations=[
            dict(text="2% target", xref="paper", yref="y", x=1.01, y=2,
                 xanchor="left", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=11, color="#888")),
            dict(text="The Bank of Canada targets inflation of 2%, within a control range of 1–3%.",
                 xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, yanchor="top", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999")),
            dict(text=_src,
                 xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.42, yanchor="top", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999")),
        ])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("CPI data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## How much prices have risen

Inflation — the *rate* at which prices rise — has cooled back toward the Bank of Canada's target. But a lower inflation rate does not mean prices have fallen; it means they are climbing more slowly from a much higher level. That gap is a big part of why budgets still feel stretched. Since 2019, **overall prices are about a quarter higher**, and the essentials households feel most have risen even more — **food and shelter are each up roughly a third**. This shows the price *level* of the all-items basket and the most-felt categories, rebased so 2019 = 100.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    comp = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi_components.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    comp["cpi_value"] = pd.to_numeric(comp["cpi_value"], errors="coerce")
    base = comp[comp["date"].dt.year == 2019].groupby("product_group")["cpi_value"].mean()
    comp = comp[comp["date"] >= "2019-01-01"].copy()
    comp["idx"] = [r.cpi_value / base[r.product_group] * 100 for r in comp.itertuples()]
    series = [("All-items", "#555555", 3.2), ("Food", CATEGORICAL_COLORS[1], 2.5),
              ("Shelter", CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0], 2.5), ("Transportation", CATEGORICAL_COLORS[3], 2.5)]
    figl = go.Figure()
    for name, color, w in series:
        s = comp[comp["product_group"] == name].sort_values("date")
        figl.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=s["date"], y=s["idx"], name=name, mode="lines",
            line=dict(color=color, width=w), hovertemplate=name + ": %{y:.0f}<extra></extra>"))
    figl.add_hline(y=100, line_dash="dot", line_color="#aaa")
    figl.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="Price level (2019 = 100)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.6)"),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=80, r=20))
    figl.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0004-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/economics/statcan_cpi_components.csv')}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.18, xanchor="left", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figl.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("CPI component data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## Has pay kept up?

The other half of cost-of-living pressure is whether earnings keep pace. This compares the **average weekly wage** with the all-items price level, both rebased to 2019 = 100. On average, wages have slightly outpaced prices since the pandemic — but that average hides a lot: pay rose unevenly across workers, and it lagged behind the price surge of 2021–22 before catching up, which is much of why the squeeze felt acute even as the totals roughly balanced.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    wp = pd.read_csv("data/income/statcan_wages_by_province.csv")
    wp = wp[wp["geo"] == "Canada"].set_index("year")["avg_weekly_wage"]
    ci = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    if "product_group" in ci.columns:
        ci = ci[ci["product_group"] == "All-items"]
    ci = ci.assign(yr=ci["date"].dt.year).groupby("yr")["cpi_value"].mean()
    base = 2019
    yrs = [y for y in wp.index if y >= base and y in ci.index]
    figw = go.Figure()
    figw.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=yrs, y=[ci[y] / ci[base] * 100 for y in yrs], name="Prices (all-items)",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[1], width=2.5), hovertemplate="Prices: %{y:.0f}<extra></extra>"))
    figw.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=yrs, y=[wp[y] / wp[base] * 100 for y in yrs], name="Average weekly wage",
        mode="lines+markers", line=dict(color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0], width=3), marker=dict(size=6),
        hovertemplate="Wage: %{y:.0f}<extra></extra>"))
    figw.add_hline(y=100, line_dash="dot", line_color="#aaa")
    figw.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=440, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="Index (2019 = 100)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", dtick=1),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=80, r=20))
    figw.add_annotation(text=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Tables 14-10-0064-01 (wages) and 18-10-0004-01 (CPI) | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/income/statcan_wages_by_province.csv')}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.2, xanchor="left", showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figw.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("Wage/CPI data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Headline, core, and food

Three views of the same question. **All-items** is the headline rate; **CPI-trim** is the Bank of Canada's preferred *core* measure, which strips out the most volatile price movements to show the underlying trend; **food** is the most-felt component, and has generally run hotter than overall inflation.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    ai = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    if "product_group" in ai.columns:
        ai = ai[ai["product_group"] == "All-items"]
    ai = ai.sort_values("date")
    ai["yoy"] = pd.to_numeric(ai["cpi_value"], errors="coerce").pct_change(12) * 100
    fd = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_food_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"]).sort_values("date")
    fd["yoy"] = pd.to_numeric(fd["food_index"], errors="coerce").pct_change(12) * 100
    tr = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi_trim.csv", parse_dates=["date"]).sort_values("date")

    figc = go.Figure()
    figc.add_hrect(y0=1, y1=3, fillcolor="#e0e0e0", opacity=0.35, line_width=0, layer="below")
    figc.add_hline(y=2, line_dash="dash", line_color="#888", opacity=0.6)
    figc.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=ai["date"], y=ai["yoy"], name="All-items", mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=3), hovertemplate="All-items: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    figc.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=tr["date"], y=tr["cpi_trim_yoy"], name="CPI-trim (core)", mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[0], width=2), hovertemplate="CPI-trim: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    figc.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=fd["date"], y=fd["yoy"], name="Food", mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[1], width=2), hovertemplate="Food: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    latest = ai["date"].max()
    figc.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, hovermode="x unified",
        meta=dict(autoscaleY=dict(include=[0, 3], padFrac=0.08)),
        yaxis=dict(title="Year-over-year change (%)", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%",
                   range=[-3, 13]),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", range=[pd.Timestamp("2015-01-01"), latest],
            rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                dict(count=5, label="5Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        legend=dict(yanchor="top", y=0.98, xanchor="left", x=0.02, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.6)"),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=120, r=20))
    figc.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Statistics Canada 18-10-0004-01 (all-items, food) and 18-10-0256-01 (CPI-trim) | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/economics/statcan_cpi_trim.csv')}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figc.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("Core/food CPI data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## Where inflation is coming from

Inflation is an average across a basket of goods and services, but the pieces move at very different speeds. This shows the 12-month change in each of the eight major components of the Consumer Price Index — which corners of the basket are running hot and which are cool. It is the *rate* each component is rising, not its weight in the headline figure (shelter and food carry the most weight in the average); the all-items rate is marked in grey for reference.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    comp = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi_components.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    comp["cpi_value"] = pd.to_numeric(comp["cpi_value"], errors="coerce")
    comp = comp.sort_values(["product_group", "date"])
    comp["yoy"] = comp.groupby("product_group")["cpi_value"].pct_change(12) * 100
    latest = comp["date"].max()
    cur = comp[comp["date"] == latest].dropna(subset=["yoy"]).copy()
    short = {
        "Household operations, furnishings and equipment": "Household operations & furnishings",
        "Recreation, education and reading": "Recreation, education & reading",
        "Alcoholic beverages, tobacco products and recreational cannabis": "Alcohol, tobacco & cannabis",
        "Health and personal care": "Health & personal care",
        "Clothing and footwear": "Clothing & footwear",
    }
    cur["label"] = cur["product_group"].map(lambda p: short.get(p, p))
    cur["lbl"] = [f"{v:+.1f}%" for v in cur["yoy"]]
    fig = category_bar(cur, "label", "yoy",
        xaxis_title="12-month change (%)", ticksuffix="%",
        highlight={"All-items"}, highlight_color="#555",
        text_col="lbl", text_fmt="{}",
        hovertemplate="%{y}: %{x:+.1f}%<extra></extra>",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada 18-10-0004-01 | {latest:%B %Y}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("CPI components data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## Food prices, by category

Food is the component households feel most — but "food" runs from meat to restaurant meals, and these have risen at very different rates. This breaks the food basket into its major grocery categories plus restaurant meals. **Use the dropdown** to switch between the change over the past year and over the past five years; the longer window captures the post-2021 surge that lifted most grocery categories by roughly a quarter to a third.

```{python}
#| echo: false
food_path = "data/economics/statcan_cpi_food_components.csv"
if os.path.exists(food_path):
    fc = pd.read_csv(food_path, parse_dates=["date"]).sort_values(["food_group", "date"])
    flatest = fc["date"].max()
    def _food_chg(months):
        rows = []
        for g, s in fc.groupby("food_group"):
            ser = s.set_index("date")["cpi_value"]
            past = ser[ser.index <= flatest - pd.DateOffset(months=months)]
            if flatest in ser.index and len(past) and past.iloc[-1] > 0:
                rows.append({"food_group": g, "chg": (ser.loc[flatest] / past.iloc[-1] - 1) * 100})
        d = pd.DataFrame(rows)
        d["lbl"] = [f"{v:+.1f}%" for v in d["chg"]]
        return d
    fig = category_bar_views(
        [("Past 5 years", _food_chg(60)), ("Past 12 months", _food_chg(12))],
        "food_group", "chg", xaxis_title="Price change (%)",
        value_fmt=".1f", ticksuffix="%", color=CATEGORICAL_COLORS[1],
        text_col="lbl", text_fmt="{}",
        source_note=f"Source: Statistics Canada, Table 18-10-0004-01 | {flatest:%B %Y}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Food component data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## The policy rate vs. inflation

The Bank of Canada raises its **policy interest rate** to cool inflation and lowers it to support the economy — so the two move together over time. This pairs the policy rate (the **Bank Rate**, the consistent series back to 1935) with year-over-year inflation, both on one scale. The early-1980s spike — the rate above **20%** against double-digit inflation — and the 2022–23 tightening cycle stand out. The grey band is the 1–3% inflation target.

```{python}
#| echo: false
try:
    pr = pd.read_csv("data/economics/boc_policy_rate.csv", parse_dates=["date"]).sort_values("date")
    ci = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    if "product_group" in ci.columns:
        ci = ci[ci["product_group"] == "All-items"]
    ci = ci.sort_values("date")
    ci["yoy"] = pd.to_numeric(ci["cpi_value"], errors="coerce").pct_change(12) * 100
    ci = ci.dropna(subset=["yoy"])
    ci = ci[ci["date"] >= pr["date"].min()]   # align both lines to the rate's start (1935)

    figr = go.Figure()
    figr.add_hrect(y0=1, y1=3, fillcolor="#e0e0e0", opacity=0.35, line_width=0, layer="below")
    figr.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=ci["date"], y=ci["yoy"], name="Inflation (CPI, year-over-year)",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color=CANADA_COLOR, width=2),
        hovertemplate="Inflation: %{y:.1f}%<extra></extra>"))
    figr.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=pr["date"], y=pr["policy_rate"], name="Policy rate (Bank Rate)",
        mode="lines", line=dict(color="#1f77b4", width=2.5),
        hovertemplate="Policy rate: %{y:.2f}%<extra></extra>"))
    figr.update_layout(plot_bgcolor="white", height=460, hovermode="x unified",
        yaxis=dict(title="Percent", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%", zeroline=True, zerolinecolor="#ccc"),
        xaxis=dict(title="", gridcolor="#e0e0e0",
            rangeselector=dict(buttons=[
                dict(count=10, label="10Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(count=20, label="20Y", step="year", stepmode="backward"),
                dict(step="all", label="All")], x=0, xanchor="left", y=1.01),
            rangeslider=dict(visible=True, thickness=0.08, bgcolor="#f5f5f5")),
        legend=dict(yanchor="top", y=0.98, xanchor="right", x=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.6)"),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=120, r=20))
    figr.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: Bank of Canada (Bank Rate) and Statistics Canada 18-10-0004-01 | Data as of: {get_data_date('data/economics/boc_policy_rate.csv')}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figr.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("Policy-rate data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## The minimum wage, nominal and real

Minimum wages are set by each province and territory, so there is no single national rate. This shows the four most populous provinces. In **nominal** dollars the minimum wage only ever rises; switch to **real** to adjust for inflation, which is what tells you whether the lowest-paid are actually gaining ground. (All 14 jurisdictions, including the federal rate, are in the data download.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
mw_path = "data/economics/esdc_minimum_wage.csv"
try:
    mw = pd.read_csv(mw_path)
    cpi = pd.read_csv("data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv", parse_dates=["date"])
    if "product_group" in cpi.columns:
        cpi = cpi[cpi["product_group"] == "All-items"]
    cpi["yr"] = cpi["date"].dt.year
    cpi_annual = cpi.groupby("yr")["cpi_value"].mean()
    full_year = cpi.groupby("yr")["date"].nunique()
    base_year = int(full_year[full_year >= 12].index.max())   # latest complete CPI year
    base_cpi = cpi_annual[base_year]
    PROVS = ["Ontario", "Quebec", "British Columbia", "Alberta"]
    m = mw[(mw["jurisdiction"].isin(PROVS)) & (mw["year"] <= base_year)].copy()
    m["real"] = m["min_wage"] * base_cpi / m["year"].map(cpi_annual)
    m = m.dropna(subset=["real"])
    fig = lines_over_time(m, "year", "min_wage", "jurisdiction",
        yaxis_title="$ per hour", group_order=PROVS,
        colors=province_colors(PROVS),
        measures=[
            {"col": "min_wage", "label": "Nominal", "yaxis_title": "$ per hour (nominal)",
             "ytickprefix": "$", "hover_decimals": 2},
            {"col": "real", "label": f"Real ({base_year} $)",
             "yaxis_title": f"$ per hour (constant {base_year} $)", "ytickprefix": "$", "hover_decimals": 2},
        ],
        source_note=f"Source: ESDC, Historical Minimum Wage Rates in Canada (open.canada.ca); deflated by StatCan all-items CPI | Data as of: {get_data_date(mw_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("Minimum-wage data not yet available. Run the data pipeline to fetch it.")
```

## Other cost pressures

Inflation is only part of the cost-of-living picture. See also:

- [Housing](../housing/) — house prices, the price-to-income gap, rent, and household debt
- [Income & Inequality](../income/) — wages, median income, and low-income and food-insecurity rates

## Download data

- [CPI / Inflation (CSV)](../data/economics/statcan_cpi.csv) — Statistics Canada 18-10-0004-01
- [Minimum wage by jurisdiction (CSV)](../data/economics/esdc_minimum_wage.csv) — ESDC, Historical Minimum Wage Rates in Canada
- [Food CPI (CSV)](../data/economics/statcan_food_cpi.csv) — Statistics Canada 18-10-0004-01
- [CPI by major component (CSV)](../data/economics/statcan_cpi_components.csv) — Statistics Canada 18-10-0004-01
- [CPI-trim core inflation (CSV)](../data/economics/statcan_cpi_trim.csv) — Statistics Canada 18-10-0256-01
- [Bank of Canada policy rate (CSV)](../data/economics/boc_policy_rate.csv) — Bank of Canada (Bank Rate)
